critic_raw:
This experiment tests a CNN for early-stage lung cancer detection against radiologist consensus sensitivity.

The comparison between a CNN and radiologist consensus sensitivity is fundamentally asymmetric in threshold selection. The CNN's decision threshold is selected on the validation set 'to match or exceed the radiologist consensus sensitivity.' This means the CNN's threshold is chosen specifically to perform well on the sensitivity dimension, while radiologists apply their own clinical judgment across all cases without threshold tuning. A model whose threshold is optimized to achieve target sensitivity will by design match or exceed that sensitivity — the question is whether this is achieved at comparable or better specificity. If the CNN achieves the same sensitivity as radiologists but at substantially lower specificity, the comparison is not 'CNN achieves higher sensitivity' but rather 'CNN operates at a different precision-recall tradeoff point.' The threshold selection procedure ensures the sensitivity comparison is favorable to the CNN by construction.

The baseline is 'radiologist consensus on the same test set.' Radiologists reading the test set images are exposed to images from 2022–2023. If the CNN is trained on images from the same institution with similar acquisition protocols, the CNN may be performing image pattern matching on known-site characteristics rather than generalizing lung cancer features. The radiologists may not have this advantage (they may have trained at different institutions). The comparison therefore potentially pits a site-specific pattern matcher against human visual recognition that was not specifically calibrated to this site's image acquisition protocol.

The model selection process evaluates 'several CNN architectures (ResNet-50 with ImageNet pretraining, a custom 3D volumetric CNN, or a Vision Transformer) on the validation set and select the best performer.' This is architecture search on the validation set. With 1,500 validation images, comparing 3+ architectures and selecting the best validation performer introduces selection bias — the test set evaluation of the winning architecture will be optimistically biased. A proper multiple-comparison correction (e.g., Bonferroni on validation performance, or a separate test set per architecture) would be needed to avoid this.

With 10,000 total images and a 15% test set (1,500 images), the number of positive (early-stage cancer) cases in the test set depends on prevalence. Early-stage lung cancer in an archival CT dataset is likely enriched relative to population prevalence (radiologists tend to flag suspected cases for archiving). If the archival rate is 20%, there are 300 positive test cases. Sensitivity estimates on 300 cases have 95% CI width of approximately ±5 percentage points — adequate for detecting a 10-point sensitivity advantage but marginal for detecting a 5-point advantage, which may be the clinically relevant threshold.
defender_raw:
The lung cancer CNN detection design makes a critical methodological investment in label quality that most medical imaging studies do not: the ground truth is established by consensus of two independent radiologists, with third-expert tiebreaking, and then confirmed by histopathology or clinical follow-up of ≥24 months for negative cases. This multi-level label construction directly addresses the circular problem that exists when a model is compared against the same radiologists whose opinions define the labels. By establishing a consensus ground truth that is then independently verified, the labels become more reliable than any single radiologist opinion, and the comparison between CNN sensitivity and radiologist consensus sensitivity is testing the CNN against a stable, adjudicated standard rather than against noisy individual assessments.

However, there is an acknowledged structural tension: the radiologist consensus both partially defines the ground truth and serves as the performance baseline. The design's use of histopathology confirmation and 24-month follow-up for negative cases substantially mitigates this tension by grounding the labels in objective clinical outcomes beyond radiologist assessment. The key defense is that the design does not use the same radiologist opinions as both label and baseline without an independent anchor — the pathology confirmation provides that anchor.

The stratified split preserving institution and disease prevalence is important for a multi-site archive. Without institution stratification, a high-volume site with a particular scanner protocol could dominate one split and create performance estimates that reflect equipment characteristics rather than anatomical feature learning. The patient-level split prevents data leakage from multi-scan patients.

Sensitivity is the correct primary metric for early-stage cancer detection. The clinical cost of a missed diagnosis (delayed treatment, worse prognosis) is substantially higher than the cost of a false positive (biopsy recommendation, anxiety). Optimizing sensitivity with a specificity floor reflects this asymmetric cost structure.

The sensitivity analysis excluding the highest-volume institution tests whether the result is driven by one site's scanner artifacts, which is exactly the right check for a multi-site model claiming generalizability.
all_issues_raised: The CNN's decision threshold is selected on validation to match or exceed radiologist sensitivity, ensuring the CNN meets the sensitivity target by construction; this procedure makes the sensitivity comparison circular and does not test whether the CNN is genuinely better, only whether it can be calibrated to match radiologists at some specificity cost.

Architecture selection across ResNet-50, 3D volumetric CNN, and Vision Transformer on the validation set introduces selection bias that inflates the apparent performance of the winning architecture on the test set; no multiple-comparison correction is applied.

The CNN trained on archival images from the same institution may exploit site-specific acquisition characteristics that radiologists do not have calibrated to, making the comparison between CNN and radiologists not a fair test of diagnostic capability.

With approximately 300 positive cases in the 1,500-image test set (assuming 20% cancer prevalence in an archival dataset), sensitivity estimates have ±5 percentage point 95% CIs, which is marginal for detecting the clinically relevant sensitivity differences.
all_issues_adjudicated: Architecture selection across ResNet-50, 3D volumetric CNN, and Vision Transformer on the validation set introduces selection bias that inflates the apparent performance of the winning architecture on the test set; no multiple-comparison correction is applied.

With approximately 300 positive cases in the 1,500-image test set (assuming 20% cancer prevalence in an archival dataset), sensitivity estimates have ±5 percentage point 95% CIs, which is marginal for detecting the clinically relevant sensitivity differences.
